title: This Is the Most Detailed Image Yet of the Milky Way's Center
gist: ESA's Euclid space telescope captured the largest and most detailed visible-light image of the Milky Way's galactic bulge—60 million stars in 26 hours across nine pointings—which will serve as a baseline reference archive enabling the upcoming Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope to measure planetary masses for future microlensing exoplanet discoveries.

insights.claim: Euclid's visible-light camera captures an area 270 times larger than Hubble's field of view in a single pointing, completing in a few hours an observation that would take the Keck Observatory approximately 2,000 hours—establishing Euclid as the dominant wide-field space imaging instrument.
insights.speaker: Marta Musso
insights.so_what: Euclid's field-of-view advantage is orders of magnitude over existing observatories; suggests rapid scientific ROI from ESA's investment and positions Euclid data as a platform for many downstream scientific programs.
insights.quote: the Keck Observatory would require roughly 2,000 hours to observe the same mosaic
insights.locator: Each pointing that Euclid captures in just a few hours

insights.claim: Euclid captured 60 million stars in the Milky Way's galactic bulge—the galaxy's most crowded region—in just 26 hours across nine pointings on March 23, 2025, without being overwhelmed by the intense stellar light.
insights.speaker: Marta Musso
insights.so_what: Successful crowded-field galactic-bulge imaging validates Euclid's dynamic range performance; the dataset is immediately scientifically valuable for exoplanet and stellar research.
insights.locator: On March 23, 2025, Euclid turned its gaze toward the galactic bulge

themes.theme: Roman Space Telescope synergy and exoplanet science

insights.claim: Euclid's galactic bulge image includes 51 known planetary systems and will enable researchers to measure the masses of planets detected by the upcoming Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope via microlensing, because Euclid captured stellar positions before any microlensing events occur—functioning as a historical baseline.
insights.speaker: Marta Musso
insights.so_what: Euclid's archive will multiply the scientific output of Roman's exoplanet mission without additional observation time—a powerful example of multi-mission synergy in space science that maximizes each mission's ROI.
insights.quote: In 24 hours, Euclid has already captured the stars involved in all the future microlensing events that the Roman space telescope will detect, but before the stars and planets involved have aligned
insights.locator: said Natalia Rektsini, who led the publication of the data

insights.claim: Almost 300 exoplanets have been discovered via gravitational microlensing over 20 years, all by ground-based telescopes targeting the galactic center; Euclid's image now provides the systematic baseline data needed to dramatically accelerate this work with space-based instruments.
insights.speaker: Marta Musso
insights.so_what: Space-based microlensing surveys (Roman + Euclid) will produce a surge in exoplanet mass characterization over the next decade; the galactic center exoplanet census is about to accelerate rapidly.
insights.locator: During the last 20 years, almost 300 exoplanets have been discovered using this technique

insights.claim: Euclid's Milky Way galactic bulge dataset is simultaneously being applied to studies of brown dwarfs, binary stars, stellar motions, and dust distribution across the galaxy—demonstrating broad scientific utility extending well beyond its primary cosmological mission.
insights.speaker: Marta Musso
insights.so_what: Multi-use datasets maximize ROI from space missions; Euclid's galactic survey is a platform dataset whose value compounds as researchers apply new techniques across stellar and planetary science.
insights.quote: This data can also be used for other scientific applications, from brown dwarfs and binary stars to stellar motions and dust across our galaxy.
insights.locator: said Valeria Pettorino, ESA's Euclid project scientist
